title: Interplay between photon condensation and electron-electron interactions in molecular systems
authors: Matteo Parisi, Elisabetta Paladino, Giuseppe A. Falci, Gian Marcello Andolina, Salvatore Savasta, Marco Polini, Francesco M. D. Pellegrino
abstract: We investigate a minimal molecular model consisting of square planar plaquettes hosting multiple electrons, whose dynamics is governed by a tight-binding Hamiltonian supplemented by on-site Hubbard repulsion. By coupling this system to a spatially nonuniform cavity mode, we analyze the emergence of a magnetostatic instability, namely photon condensation, originating from the paramagnetic Van Vleck mechanism. The global behavior of the system is analyzed for different electronic filling factors, and we find that, except for the special cases of half-filling and single electron, where the transition, if it occurs, is necessarily a second order phase transition, the global system may also undergo a first order transition because of the action of the electron-electron interaction. The polaritonic excitation energies are analyzed, providing clear spectroscopic signatures of the magnetostatic instability and of its order.
summary: This paper models photon condensation in molecular systems by coupling electrons to an electromagnetic cavity mode. It rigorously incorporates on-site Coulomb repulsion via the Hubbard term, demonstrating that this interaction can drive the system into a first-order phase transition regime, contrasting with simpler non-interacting predictions.
main_problem: The study investigates the complex interplay between photon condensation (a magnetostatic instability) and electron-electron interactions within molecular quantum systems.
main_result: The inclusion of Hubbard repulsion can fundamentally change the nature of the phase transition, allowing for a first-order transition in regimes where non-interacting models predict only second-order transitions.
method: The analysis employs mean-field theory coupled with exact diagonalization techniques on small molecular plaquettes to analyze energy functionals and order parameters.
model_or_system: The model consists of multiple identical square planar plaquettes hosting electrons, described by a tight-binding Hamiltonian augmented by an on-site Hubbard repulsion term ($U$). This system is coupled to a spatially nonuniform cavity mode.
key_observables: Photon condensate order parameter ($ ar{\alpha}$), polaritonic excitation energies, and the character (first vs. second order) of the phase transition.
important_parameters: Electron filling factor ($N_e$), Hubbard repulsion strength ($U/t$), light-matter coupling strength ($\eta$), and hopping parameters ($	au/t$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on mean-field decoupling for light-matter coupling, and in some parts, approximations like Holstein-Primakoff transformations are used to linearize the Hamiltonian around the mean-field state.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the order parameter $ ar{\alpha}$ versus hopping ratio ($	au/t$) showing discontinuities (first-order) or smooth changes (second-order), and plot polaritonic energies revealing spectral signatures of the instability.
paper_structure: The paper builds from defining the coupled Hamiltonian, analyzing the single-molecule electronic structure using SU(2) symmetry, deriving a mean-field effective Hamiltonian for light-matter coupling, and finally determining the phase transition order by examining energy functionals and polaritonic spectra.
why_interesting: This work directly addresses cavity QED phenomena (photon condensation/superradiance) in realistic, correlated solid-state or molecular settings, providing a detailed mechanism for how electron correlations modify fundamental quantum optical transitions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. a) Low energy spectrum for Ne = 2 of a square molecule (ns = 4), in the absence of repulsive electron- electron interaction. b) Low-energy spectrum for the same molecular system, now with U/t = 1. The vertical dashed lines at τ1 ≈−1.33t and τ2 ≈−0.72t mark the bound- aries where the ground state becomes a three-fold degener- ate triplet state. In both panels, red solid lines represent the eigenenergies of the singlet states (S = 0), while the black dashed lines refer to triplet states (S = 1).

FIG. 2. The lowest eigenenergy, for a square molecular pla- quette with ns = 4, as a function of τ/t, for U = 0 (red), U/t = 1 (green), U/t = 20 (blue), and U/t = 100 (magenta). a) Ne = 2 electrons per molecule, the solid lines represent the lowest eigenenergy within the subspaces S, and the minimal eigenenergy in the subspaces Ti (i = 0, ±1) is ϵ0 + ϵ1 (black dashed line), independently of i. b) Ne = 3 electrons per molecule, the solid lines indicate the lowest eigenenergy within the subspaces Di (i = ±1/2), while the minimum eigenenergy within the subspaces Qi (where i = ±1/2, ±3/2) is denoted as ϵ0 (black dashed line), independently of i. c) Half-filling case, Ne = 4, colored lines refer...

FIG. 3. Photon condensation of square molecular plaquettes with Ne = 2 electrons. a) [c)] The photon condensate order parameter ¯α is plotted as a function of τ/t [η], by setting ωc = t and η = 0.13 [τ = −t]. The red solid line denotes U/t = 1, and the green dashed line refers to U = 0. Furthermore, in c), the dashed gray vertical line at η∗≈0.17 indicates the value where ¯α becomes non-zero with a jump discontinuity. b) [d)] Low-energy spectrum of the generic k-th molecule, characterized by ˆHMF,k(¯α), is considered with ¯α determined under the conditions ωc = t, η = 0.13 [τ = −t], and U/t = 1. The red and black solid lines indicate the lowest eigenenergies in the subspaces S and T,...

FIG. 4. Photonic order parameter |¯α| as a function of τ/t and the light-matter coupling strength η, with Ne = 2 electrons and setting ωc = t. The color map represents the case with U/t = 1. The green dashed line indicates the critical val- ues of the microscopic parameters τ and η, where the phase transition occurs without the influence of electron-electron in- teraction U. The vertical white dashed lines at τ1 ≈−1.33t and τ2 ≈−0.72t denote the range [τ1, τ2], within which the ground state for η = 0 is a degenerate triplet.

FIG. 5. Photon condensation of square molecular plaquettes with Ne = 3 electrons. The photon condensate order param- eter |¯α| is plotted as a function of τ/t and U/t, by setting ωc = t and η = 0.12. The sharp boundary between the nor- mal and photon condensate phase in the upper right corner signals a first order phase transition.
